w:r.w:t: VERSION 1.0 | FOR HANDOVER TO CODING AGENT
Local WiFi (LAN) via in-app WebSocket server
Modular — transport layer abstracted for future cloud

Numra is a casual multiplayer number puzzle game inspired by the Numbers Round format popularised by the British game show Countdown. Players are given a set of numbers and a target, and must reach (or get as close as possible to) the target using basic arithmetic within a time limit.

The game is designed for social, in-person play among friends — a digital alternative to board games. It is built mobile-first and is intended to be played entirely on smartphones with no additional hardware required.
Solo practice mode for individual skill development
Local WiFi multiplayer: one phone hosts, others join via QR code scan
Solo player and team-based play within a multiplayer session
Real-time spectator support — friends can watch live without playing
Post-round solution reveal with step-by-step breakdown
Configurable game rules: number pool, target range, time limits, scoring

The underlying game mechanic (reach a target using arithmetic on a given set of numbers) is not protectable by copyright. Game mechanics are explicitly excluded from copyright law in all major jurisdictions. Numra is free to use this mechanic commercially under its own branding.
The following must be strictly avoided to maintain clean IP:
Do not use the name Countdown or any derivative
Do not reproduce the visual identity, clock, or music of the original show
Do not replicate the exact format wholesale for commercial exploitation
NOTE: Numra's modified rules and original presentation provide additional differentiation beyond the legal minimum.
A single round proceeds as follows:
Host configures and starts a round
The game engine generates a set of numbers and a target (all players see identical values, enforced by shared seed)
A countdown timer begins — default 30 seconds, configurable
Each player constructs an arithmetic expression using the given numbers
Players submit before the timer expires
Round ends: all submissions are revealed simultaneously
Scoring is applied; the solver engine reveals the optimal solution
Scores accumulate across rounds; host controls when the game ends
Default scoring mirrors the classic format but is fully configurable by the host:
In team mode, the best submission from any team member counts for the team. All scoring rules apply identically.
The submission validator enforces these rules on every expression:
Only the numbers provided in the round may be used
Each number may be used at most once (unless it appears multiple times in the drawn set)
Operations permitted: addition, subtraction, multiplication, integer division only
Division that produces a non-integer result at any intermediate step is invalid
Negative intermediate values are permitted (configurable: can be disabled by host)
All numbers in the drawn set do not need to be used
Solo practice runs entirely offline with no network involvement. Features include:
Configurable difficulty tiers (Easy / Medium / Hard / Custom)
Hint system: the engine can reveal whether a solution exists within N steps
Full solution reveal with step-by-step breakdown after round ends or time expires
Local statistics stored on device: best score, average closeness, streaks, rounds played
No account or internet connection required

The single most important architectural decision is the separation of game logic from the transport layer. The game engine must have no knowledge of how messages travel between players. This separation is what allows the same codebase to support LAN play today and cloud-based play in the future without touching the game logic.
│ Pure Dart — no Flutter, no I/O │
Swapping from LAN to cloud multiplayer means replacing only the transport implementation. The game engine, all UI screens, and all business logic remain completely unchanged.
All transport implementations must conform to this interface. The game engine interacts only with IGameTransport and never with concrete implementations.

The hosting device runs a lightweight WebSocket server inside the Flutter app using the Dart shelf and shelf_web_socket packages. Other devices on the same WiFi network connect to this server as WebSocket clients. No router configuration, port forwarding, or external service is required.

All devices — players and spectators — must be connected to the same WiFi network. This is the only hard infrastructure requirement for local multiplayer. It is unavoidable regardless of technology choice and is a natural constraint of a LAN party context.
│ └── QR code display (encodes IP + port + session ID) │
Player Phone Player Phone Spectator Phone
WS client) WS client) WS client,
The QR code encodes a JSON payload. Scanning it gives the joining device everything it needs to connect — no manual IP entry, no name discovery screen, no waiting for broadcast scans.
The host can regenerate the QR code at any time (e.g. to allow a late joiner or to rotate the session token). QR generation uses the qr_flutter package; scanning uses mobile_scanner.

Apple restricts background processes on iOS. The in-app shelf server is a foreground process and is permitted. The practical constraint is: the host device must keep the app open and the screen active for the duration of the game. This is natural behaviour for a host and requires no special handling beyond a screen wake lock (available via wakelock_plus).

Spectators receive the full event stream from the server. The server ignores any PlayerEvent messages emitted by a spectator-role connection. This is enforced server-side — there is no client-side trust. The spectator role is set at join time and cannot be changed mid-session.

The game engine is a pure Dart package with no Flutter dependencies and no I/O. It can be tested in isolation with a standard dart test suite. It exposes a clean API that the UI layer and transport layer call into.
Orchestrates the round lifecycle: lobby → countdown → play → reveal → scores. Acts as the state machine.
Selects numbers from configurable pools using a seeded RNG. Same seed produces identical draws on all devices.
Generates the target number within a configurable range using the same seed as NumberGenerator.
Parses and validates a player expression: correct numbers used, no over-use, integer division only, result computed correctly.
Finds the optimal or near-optimal solution via recursive tree search. Runs server-side (on host) to avoid client trust issues. Result broadcast at round end.
Accumulates scores across rounds. Supports individual and team scoring. Scoring rules are injected as configuration, not hardcoded.
The host generates a random seed at the start of each round and broadcasts it to all players. Each client then independently generates the numbers and target from that seed. This means:
The game never sends the numbers themselves over the network — only the seed
All clients are guaranteed to see identical numbers without any trust in client-reported values
The host cannot manipulate numbers mid-round
Rounds are fully reproducible for debugging or replay

The solver finds the closest achievable result to the target using the drawn numbers. It uses a recursive combinatorial search — trying all permutations of subsets of numbers and all combinations of operators and operator precedences (modelled as all possible binary expression trees).

For 6 numbers, the search space is large but tractable on modern hardware in under one second. The solver runs on the host device after round end and the result is broadcast to all players. Clients do not run the solver — only the host does. This prevents timing discrepancies.
The solver result includes the full step-by-step breakdown, e.g.:
LOBBY ──► COUNTDOWN ──► IN_PLAY ──► REVEAL ──► SCORES ──► LOBBY
Players join, set names, form teams. Host configures round parameters. Host triggers start.
3-second visual countdown before numbers are shown. Builds tension.
Numbers and target visible. Timer running. Players submit expressions.
All submissions shown simultaneously. Solver result broadcast. Scores calculated.
Leaderboard shown. Host can start next round or end game.
Final scores shown. Option to play again (returns to LOBBY) or exit.
6.1 Game Events (Host to All — Players and Spectators)
RoundStart({seed, duration_ms}) // clients derive numbers from seed
ScoresUpdate({scores}) // per player and per team
NOTE: Spectators may not emit PlayerEvents. The server silently discards any PlayerEvent received from a spectator-role connection.
All WebSocket messages are JSON-encoded with the following envelope:

The web build is the first build target for Numra. The reason is pragmatic: deploying a Flutter web app requires no app store submission, no device provisioning, no TestFlight or Play Store beta. A single flutter build web produces static files that can be hosted anywhere (GitHub Pages, Firebase Hosting, Netlify) and accessed immediately from any browser on any device.

The web build is used exclusively for gameplay playtesting — to validate that the core game loop, pacing, scoring, and UX feel right before committing to mobile store builds. Once the gameplay is validated, the identical codebase is built for Android and iOS with no logic changes.
7.2 What Works Natively in Flutter Web
All game engine logic — pure Dart, runs in the browser via dart2js compilation
WebSocket client connections — fully supported in browser environments
All UI screens and widgets — Flutter renders identically on web
QR code generation (qr_flutter works on web)
Local storage for practice stats (shared_preferences uses localStorage on web)
7.3 Web Constraint — Hosting the LAN Server
Flutter web runs inside a browser sandbox. A browser tab cannot open a raw TCP socket or run a WebSocket server. This means the web build cannot act as the host for a LAN multiplayer session.

This is handled cleanly with a role restriction: on web, the host role is disabled. Web players can join an existing session hosted by a mobile device, or play solo. For playtesting purposes, one person runs the mobile build (Android APK sideloaded or iOS via TestFlight) to host, while others join from their browsers.
NOTE: This constraint disappears entirely in the future cloud transport — web players will be full participants including host capability via the cloud backend.
* Web hosting limitation is by browser sandbox design, not a Flutter limitation.
For zero-friction playtesting, deploy the Flutter web build to Firebase Hosting or GitHub Pages. Both are free for static files and provide an HTTPS URL that can be shared with testers. Steps:
Deploy the build/web directory to Firebase Hosting or GitHub Pages
Share the URL — testers open it in Chrome or Safari on any device
One person with a sideloaded APK hosts the session; web users join by scanning QR

Mobile builds are the second phase, undertaken after gameplay is validated through the web playtesting build. No mobile-specific code changes are anticipated — the codebase is identical. The platform differences are in the build toolchain only.
Build command: flutter build apk --release (sideload) or flutter build appbundle (Play Store)
Minimum SDK: Android 5.0 (API 21) — covers 99%+ of active Android devices
No special permissions needed beyond INTERNET and ACCESS_WIFI_STATE for LAN hosting
Camera permission required for QR scanning (requested at runtime)
Wakelock permission required for host (screen stays on) — WAKE_LOCK in manifest
Requires Xcode on macOS, or use Codemagic CI (cloud build service, free tier available)
Camera permission: NSCameraUsageDescription required in Info.plist for QR scanning
Network permission: NSLocalNetworkUsageDescription required for LAN server discovery
Wakelock: available via wakelock_plus package (uses UIApplication.shared.isIdleTimerDisabled)
In-app foreground WebSocket server is permitted by Apple — host must keep app in foreground
│ ├── game_engine/ ← Pure Dart. No Flutter. No I/O.
│ └── transport_cloud/ ← stub for future Supabase/cloud impl
Targets web, Android, iOS from one codebase
Strong typing, null safety, excellent async support
Compile-safe, no BuildContext dependency, works on web
Official Dart packages, runs in-app on mobile
Works on all Flutter platforms including web
Practice stats; uses localStorage on web
Keeps screen on for host device during session
Gets device IP for QR payload generation
Free static hosting for playtesting builds
Cloud build avoids need for local Mac
The cloud transport is a second implementation of IGameTransport. Nothing else in the codebase changes. The game engine, all UI screens, all event types, and all game logic are identical.

Supabase is the recommended backend for the cloud transport. It provides WebSocket-based Realtime channels, a Postgres database for persistence, authentication, and Edge Functions for server-authoritative logic — all on a generous free tier suitable for early non-local multiplayer.
// joinSession() → subscribe to Supabase Realtime channel

The spectator feature works identically on cloud transport. Spectators subscribe to the same Realtime channel as players. Their role is encoded in their join metadata. The server (Edge Function or RLS policy) rejects any PlayerEvent from a spectator-role subscriber.
Build in this order. Each step produces a testable, runnable output before proceeding to the next.

game_engine package — implement and test NumberGenerator, TargetGenerator, SubmissionValidator, SolverEngine, ScoreKeeper, RoundManager in isolation. This package must have 100% coverage before any UI or transport work begins.

transport_interface package — define IGameTransport, all event types (GameEvent, PlayerEvent), and all model classes (SessionInfo, PlayerInfo, TeamInfo, Submission, SolverResult, GameConfig, ScoreMap). This is the shared contract.

Solo practice mode in the Flutter app — wire game_engine directly to the UI with NullTransport (a no-op IGameTransport implementation). This validates the UI and game engine integration before any networking.
transport_lan package — implement LanHostTransport (shelf server) and LanClientTransport (WebSocket client). Write integration tests using a loopback connection.
Multiplayer screens — host lobby, QR display, join screen (QR scanner), game screen with live player list, spectator screen, results screen.
Web build validation — run flutter build web, deploy to Firebase Hosting or GitHub Pages, validate all features work in browser.
Android build — run flutter build apk, sideload, validate LAN hosting works from a physical Android device.
The game engine package must have zero Flutter dependencies and zero I/O. It must be testable with dart test only.

The game engine must never import from transport_lan or transport_cloud. Dependency direction is one-way: UI → game_engine, UI → transport, transport → transport_interface, game_engine → transport_interface (for event types only).
All WebSocket messages must use the JSON envelope defined in Section 6.3.
The solver runs only on the host device, never on clients.
Spectator role enforcement must be server-side. Do not rely on client behaviour.
Use Riverpod for all state management. Do not use Provider, Bloc, or setState for anything beyond local widget state.
Use json_serializable for all JSON serialisation. Do not hand-write toJson/fromJson methods.
Use the seeded RNG pattern — host broadcasts seed, clients derive numbers independently.
12.3 Things to Validate During Web Playtesting
These are the gameplay questions the web build needs to answer. The coding agent does not answer these — the human product owner does after playtesting:
Is 30 seconds the right default time limit?
Is the default scoring (10 / 7 / 5 / 0) appropriately weighted?
Does the expression input UX feel natural on mobile-sized screens?
Is the solver reveal satisfying or frustrating?
Should the game show a live indicator when a player has submitted?
What is the right number of rounds for a complete game session?
Does the team mode add fun or complexity in a casual setting?
12.4 What is Out of Scope for v1
Cloud/internet multiplayer (transport_cloud is a stub only)
Custom number pool editor (host selects from presets only in v1)
Replay or game history beyond the current session
Desktop builds (Windows / macOS / Linux) — Flutter supports these but they are not a target
